topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee was scalding, a bitter, unwelcome companion to the churning in my stomach. I stared at the faded photograph on the table—us, grinning like idiots on a swing set, the summer sun bleaching the edges. It felt like a physical blow, the air in the small apartment suddenly too thin to breathe. I kept running my thumb over the glossy surface, a frantic, repetitive motion. When Liam finally walked in, whistling off-key, I jumped, nearly knocking over my mug. "Hey," he said, oblivious. "What are you staring at?" My throat tightened. I tried to form a coherent sentence, but the words caught, lodging somewhere behind my ribs. I just shook my head, a jerky, involuntary movement, and retreated to the corner, pressing my hands against my temples as the world seemed to tilt on its axis.

[story 2]

It started as a dull ache, a persistent buzzing behind my eyes that escalated quickly into a full-body tremor. I paced the length of the living room, my movements sharp and uncoordinated. Every shadow seemed to shift, every mundane sound—the distant traffic, the refrigerator humming—was amplified into a deafening roar. I kept catching my reflection in the darkened window, searching for some sign of the panic, but all I saw was a stranger with wide, frantic eyes. When I finally cornered Liam by the doorway, my voice came out thin and reedy. "Remember the treehouse?" I whispered, the sound catching on a sudden sob. He blinked slowly, a look of gentle confusion washing over his face. "Uh, no, I don't think so," he murmured, completely earnest. The realization hit me like a physical impact, leaving me breathless and unable to move.

[story 3]

My hands were shaking so violently I had to grip the edge of the counter until my knuckles were white and painful. I kept replaying the last conversation, dissecting every casual remark, searching for the sliver of memory that might prove me wrong. It wasn't there. It just wasn't. A hot flush crept up my neck, making my skin prickle. I started talking too fast, a rapid-fire stream of questions aimed at Liam, desperate for him to contradict the silence in my own mind. "We built that fort every summer! Remember the secret handshake?" I demanded, my voice rising to a pitch I couldn't control. He just looked at me, a faint, sad smile touching his lips. "I don't recall any of that," he admitted, his tone utterly placid. The floor seemed to drop out from under me. I sank onto the floor, curling into a tight ball, muffling a sound that was half-laugh, half-wail.
